aligned_pages.ppt_text:
Training Error and Generalization Error
• Training error (bias): model error on the training data
• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
• Example: practice a future exam with past exams
• Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn’t guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
• Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers

bullets.ai_comment: This slide introduces the practical challenges encountered when training neural networks, motivated by a hands-on observation: a six-layer model surprisingly underperformed a two-layer model, which contradicts the common belief that deeper networks are always more powerful. The teacher used this counterintuitive result as the central motivation for the lecture, highlighting that simply adding more layers does not guarantee better performance. The rest of the lecture is dedicated to exploring the real-world, practical factors that affect training and validation of neural networks. Understanding these practical aspects is essential for anyone building deep learning models, as theory alone is insufficient to explain or fix such issues.

generated_pages.ppt_text: Predict Who Will Repay TheirLoans •A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans •You are given complete files on 100 •5 defaulted within 3 years Image creditdebt.org

aligned_segments.text: That is, I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.
So in such a scenario, a lender helps you to investigate.
This problem.
And you are given the files of one hundred applicants.
Among these five, one hundred applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.

bullets.ai_comment: The core task here is a binary prediction problem: given information about loan applicants, determine who will repay and who will default. The teacher framed this as a real-world scenario where a lender brings in a data analyst or data scientist to solve this problem. This is a classic supervised learning use case with direct financial and risk-management implications.

bullets.ppt_text: A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher described the setup as a lender engaging you specifically to investigate the repayment behavior of applicants, positioning the learner as the analyst responsible for solving the problem. This establishes the business context: the lender needs a data-driven decision-making tool to reduce financial risk. Understanding who hired you and why is important because it grounds the machine learning task in a concrete objective.

bullets.ppt_text: You are given complete files on 100
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher clarified that the dataset consists of complete files on 100 loan applicants, meaning you have full records for each individual in the sample. This represents your training data — the historical information the model will learn patterns from. Having complete files is significant because missing data can heavily affect model quality, so a clean dataset like this is an ideal starting point for building a classifier.

The teacher highlighted that out of the 100 applicants, only 5 defaulted within three years, meaning the vast majority (95) successfully repaid their loans. This reveals a significant class imbalance in the dataset — only 5% of cases are the 'positive' (default) class. This is critically important because a naive model that always predicts 'repay' would achieve 95% accuracy yet completely fail at its real goal, illustrating why accuracy alone is a misleading metric in imbalanced classification problems.

generated_pages.ppt_text:
A Surprising Finding
• All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
• Your model may find this strong signal as well

bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces a counterintuitive scenario to illustrate a fundamental machine learning pitfall. The setup involves building a loan default prediction model, where the data reveals an unexpected pattern tied to shirt color. This surprising finding serves as a memorable entry point into discussing overfitting and the dangers of spurious correlations. Understanding why such findings are surprising — yet seductive to a model — is key to becoming a critical ML practitioner.

bullets.ppt_text: All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher describes a scenario where, out of all loan applicants, the five who defaulted happened to all be wearing blue shirts in their interview photos. This is a classic example of a spurious correlation — a pattern that exists in the data purely by coincidence, not because of any causal relationship. The teacher emphasizes that just because all defaulters wore blue shirts does not mean only blue-shirt wearers default; the observation is confounded by the tiny sample size and random chance. This kind of pattern is dangerous precisely because it looks statistically clean on the surface.

bullets.ppt_text: Your model may find this strong signal as well
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher warns that a machine learning model, lacking human common sense, will treat blue shirt color as a genuinely strong predictive signal and may even weight it heavily in its predictions. This is identified as a classic case of overfitting — the model latches onto noise in the training data rather than true underlying patterns. The teacher further points out two compounding problems: first, there is no causal evidence that shirt color predicts default behavior; second, human logic tells us shirt color is logically irrelevant to financial behavior. This example highlights why feature validation and domain knowledge are essential checks against blindly trusting what a model learns.

aligned_segments.text: And this introduce our classic discussion of training error and generalization error.
Training error means, the error on the training data set and generalization error refers to your error on a new data.
And we always say that the performance on the training data does not represents the performance on a new data set.
This is very similar to we make practices for a.

bullets.ai_comment: This slide introduces the foundational distinction between training error and generalization error, which is a classic and central topic in machine learning. The teacher frames this as a 'classic discussion,' signaling its importance in the field. Understanding the difference between these two types of errors is essential for diagnosing model performance issues such as overfitting and underfitting.

bullets.ppt_text: • Training error (bias): model error on the training data
bullets.ai_comment: Training error is the error a model makes on the very data it was trained on. The teacher explains it as 'the error on the training data set,' meaning it measures how well the model fits its own training examples. A very low training error might seem desirable, but it does not tell us how the model will perform on unseen data, which is the real goal.

bullets.ppt_text: • Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
bullets.ai_comment: Generalization error measures how well the model performs on data it has never seen before. The teacher defines it as 'your error on a new data,' emphasizing that this is the true measure of a model's usefulness in practice. A large gap between training error and generalization error is a warning sign that the model has overfit to the training data.

bullets.ppt_text: • Example: practice a future exam with past exams
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher uses the analogy of a student preparing for an exam using past exam papers. Just as a student uses past exams as practice (training data), a machine learning model trains on a known dataset. This analogy makes the abstract concept of training vs. generalization error very intuitive and relatable for students.

bullets.ppt_text: • Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn't guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher explicitly states that 'the performance on the training data does not represent the performance on a new data set,' directly mapping to this bullet. Scoring perfectly on past exams only means you have mastered those specific questions, not the underlying subject matter. This is the core danger of overfitting: optimizing for the training set at the expense of real-world performance.

bullets.ppt_text: • Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers
